subsystem: Linux kernel SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol) chunk builder. This is sctp_make_strreset_req(), the helper that constructs an outgoing SCTP RE-CONFIG chunk carrying an 'Outgoing SSN Reset Request' (SCTP_PARAM_RESET_OUT_REQUEST) and/or an 'Incoming SSN Reset Request' (SCTP_PARAM_RESET_IN_REQUEST) parameter, optionally appending an explicit list of stream identifiers (RFC 6525 stream reset). It runs in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c and serializes a user-requested stream-reset operation into wire format.
provenance: Identified by the function name sctp_make_strreset_req and the SCTP-specific symbols it uses: struct sctp_association, struct sctp_chunk, struct sctp_strreset_outreq / sctp_strreset_inreq, the SCTP_PARAM_RESET_OUT_REQUEST / SCTP_PARAM_RESET_IN_REQUEST parameter type constants, and the Linux kernel typedefs __u16 / __be16 with htons()/htonl() byte-swap helpers. sctp_make_reconf() and sctp_addto_chunk() are the SCTP chunk-allocation and chunk-append primitives in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_test_0211_vuln' and the file name cwe704 (Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast) accompany the snippet but are untrusted annotations, not evidence.
trust_boundary: The parameters stream_num (a __u16 count) and stream_list (a __be16 array of that many stream ids) originate from user space: they are copied in from a setsockopt(SCTP_RESET_STREAMS) request via sctp_send_reset_streams(), which passes the caller-supplied stream count and id array down into this builder. stream_num is therefore attacker/caller-influenced up to 65535, and any arithmetic derived from it (stream_len, outlen, inlen, the sctp_make_reconf() allocation size, and the sctp_addto_chunk() copy lengths) crosses the user->kernel trust boundary. asoc and its sequence fields are internal kernel state and are not attacker-chosen here.

what_the_code_does.code:
struct sctp_chunk *sctp_make_strreset_req(
					const struct sctp_association *asoc,
					__u16 stream_num, __be16 *stream_list,
					bool out, bool in)
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the function signature. It returns a newly built struct sctp_chunk * (or NULL on failure). asoc is the SCTP association the chunk is being built for and supplies the sequence numbers stamped into the request. stream_num is the number of stream identifiers the caller wants to reset, and stream_list points at that many big-endian (__be16) stream ids; both come from a user SCTP_RESET_STREAMS request. out and in are booleans selecting whether an outgoing-reset parameter, an incoming-reset parameter, or both are emitted. Because stream_num is a __u16 it can legitimately be as large as 65535.

stream_len is meant to hold the byte length of the stream-id list: the count stream_num multiplied by sizeof(__u16) (2 bytes per id). Note the type mismatch — sizeof yields size_t (64-bit), so the multiplication is performed in size_t, but the result is stored back into a 16-bit __u16 stream_len. outreq and inreq are stack copies of the two RFC 6525 parameter headers (sctp_strreset_outreq is param_hdr + request_seq + response_seq + send_reset_at_tsn; sctp_strreset_inreq is param_hdr + request_seq); their exact sizeof values live in headers not shown but follow the fixed SCTP wire layout (16 and 8 bytes respectively). retval is the chunk being built. outlen and inlen are also 16-bit and will hold the total serialized length of each parameter (header + stream list).

what_the_code_does.code:
outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out;
	inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in;
what_the_code_does.explanation: outlen is the full serialized length of the outgoing-reset parameter: the fixed header size sizeof(outreq) plus the stream-list byte length stream_len, multiplied by the boolean out so it is zeroed when the caller did not request an outgoing reset. inlen is the analogous length for the incoming-reset parameter. sizeof(outreq)+stream_len is computed in size_t (because sizeof is size_t) and then truncated into the 16-bit outlen/inlen. These two values are used both to size the allocation and, implicitly, to describe the parameter length on the wire.

sctp_make_reconf() allocates a RE-CONFIG chunk with room for outlen + inlen bytes of parameter payload (both operands are promoted to int for the addition). This single allocation must be large enough to hold everything appended below. If allocation fails the function returns NULL and the caller must handle it.

When outlen is non-zero the outgoing-reset parameter header is filled in: the parameter type constant, the parameter length written as network-order htons(outlen), and three sequence numbers taken from the association (the outgoing reset sequence, the last incoming sequence, and the TSN at which to apply the reset), each byte-swapped with htonl. sctp_addto_chunk(retval, sizeof(outreq), &outreq) then copies exactly sizeof(outreq) real bytes of this header into the chunk. Crucially, the length passed here is the true sizeof(outreq), not the truncated outlen.

If a stream list was requested, sctp_addto_chunk(retval, stream_len, stream_list) appends stream_len bytes of the caller-supplied stream_list into the same chunk, immediately after the outgoing-reset header. The number of bytes copied is the 16-bit stream_len computed on line 7.

Symmetric block for the incoming-reset parameter: it sets the parameter type, the network-order length htons(inlen), and a request sequence (offset by out so that if both parameters are present they use distinct sequence numbers). sctp_addto_chunk(retval, sizeof(inreq), &inreq) appends the real-sized inreq header, and, if requested, another stream_len bytes of the same stream_list. So when both out and in are set the stream list is serialized twice.

The fully populated chunk is returned to the caller (sctp_send_reset_streams path), which queues it for transmission. The correctness of every append above depends entirely on the allocation on line 16 having reserved at least sizeof(outreq)+stream_len (+ sizeof(inreq)+stream_len when in is set) bytes.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out;
	inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: outlen and inlen are 16-bit, but they must hold sizeof(outreq)+stream_len, which can reach roughly 16 + 65534 = 65550. That exceeds 65535, so the store truncates modulo 65536: e.g. with stream_len = 65534 (stream_num = 32767), sizeof(outreq)+stream_len = 65550 truncates to outlen = 14. The chunk is then allocated on line 16 for only outlen+inlen = 14 bytes. But line 27 copies the true sizeof(outreq) (~16) bytes and line 30 copies the full 16-bit stream_len (~65534) bytes into that 14-byte chunk. Because the copy lengths (sizeof(outreq), stream_len) are NOT the truncated values while the allocation IS, the append writes tens of kilobytes past the end of the allocation — a kernel heap out-of-bounds write. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that the allocation size and every appended length are computed in an integer type wide enough to hold sizeof(hdr)+stream_num*2 without truncation (compute in size_t/int and reject stream_num values whose serialized length exceeds the chunk maximum before allocating).

This is the root incorrect-type-conversion (CWE-704) defect. stream_num*sizeof(__u16) is evaluated in size_t and can be as large as 65535*2 = 131070, but is stored into the 16-bit stream_len, silently discarding the high bits (131070 & 0xFFFF = 65534; 65536 & 0xFFFF = 0). The lossy conversion means stream_len no longer equals the real byte length of the stream_list the caller supplied. This corrupts both the allocation math (line 13-14/16) and the copy length (line 30). Combined with line 13-14 it produces the heap overflow above; even in isolation it yields a chunk whose declared/copied stream-list length disagrees with stream_num. The implementation must perform this multiplication in a width that cannot truncate and must validate that stream_num is within the association's stream count before deriving any length from it.

The allocation size fed to sctp_make_reconf() is outlen + inlen, i.e. the already-truncated 16-bit lengths. Even though the addition itself is done in int, its inputs have already lost information on lines 13-14, so the chunk is undersized relative to what the subsequent sctp_addto_chunk() calls will write. There is no post-allocation bound check that the appends fit; sctp_addto_chunk() trusts the caller to have sized the chunk correctly. The invariant is that the value passed here must be the exact, non-truncated total of every byte that will later be appended (sizeof(outreq)+stream_len for the out branch plus sizeof(inreq)+stream_len for the in branch).

The parameter's on-the-wire length field is set from the truncated outlen (and inlen on line 35). When truncation has occurred, the emitted parameter advertises a length that does not match the number of bytes actually appended, producing a malformed RE-CONFIG parameter. A peer parsing this chunk will use the bogus length to bound its own copies, so the truncation can propagate a length-vs-data mismatch to the remote SCTP parser as well. The implementation must ensure the length written here equals the true serialized size and fits the 16-bit parameter-length field, rejecting requests that would not.

There is no validation in this function that stream_num (and thus the length of stream_list) is bounded to a sane value such as the number of streams negotiated for the association. The function trusts the caller entirely. If the caller path (sctp_send_reset_streams) does not itself clamp stream_num before calling in, a large user-supplied count flows straight into the truncating arithmetic on lines 7 and 13-14. The safe contract is that stream_num must be validated against asoc's outbound/inbound stream counts and against the chunk-size ceiling before any length is derived from it (missing input validation, CWE-20).

summary: sctp_make_strreset_req() derives all of its length and allocation math from a user-controlled __u16 stream count using size_t multiplications and additions that are truncated back into 16-bit variables (stream_len, outlen, inlen). Because the allocation on line 16 uses the truncated values while the sctp_addto_chunk() copies on lines 27/30/38/41 use the real sizeof() and the (also-truncated but larger) stream_len, a large stream_num makes the allocation smaller than the data copied into it, giving a kernel heap out-of-bounds write. This is an incorrect-type-conversion (CWE-704) defect that escalates to integer wraparound (CWE-190) and OOB write (CWE-787).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sctp_make_strreset_req(asoc, __u16 stream_num, __be16 *stream_list, bool out, bool in) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: stream_num/stream_list are user-supplied and unvalidated here (CWE-20).
size_t product truncated into 16-bit stream_len — root incorrect type conversion.
Stack copy of a fixed-layout parameter header; its sizeof is a constant with no attacker control. Its role in the overflow is captured under the outlen finding.
Fixed-size stack parameter header, same rationale as outreq.
Output object pointer; NULL is checked at line 17; carries no independent contract.
16-bit width is too narrow for sizeof(hdr)+stream_len — truncation drives the allocation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out; / inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in; (lines 13-14)
reviewed_anchors.reason: size_t sum truncated to 16-bit; undersizes the allocation while copies use true sizes -> heap overflow.
Allocation sized from the truncated lengths; no bound check that appends fit.
Constant assignment into a stack header field; no untrusted length.
Wire length field set from the truncated outlen -> length/data mismatch on the wire.
Byte-swapped sequence numbers from internal association state; not attacker-controlled and fixed width.
Copies the real sizeof(outreq) into an allocation sized from the truncated outlen -> part of the OOB write.
Copies up to ~65534 bytes of user stream_list into the undersized chunk -> the bulk of the OOB write.
Same truncation class as line 22 (htons(outlen)); folded into that finding rather than duplicated.
Sequence number from internal state, offset by a bool; fixed width, no overflow of concern.
Same overflow mechanism as line 27 (real sizeof into truncated allocation); covered by the outlen/inlen finding.
Second copy of the stream list; same defect and length as lines 29-30, folded into that finding.

findings.title: Heap out-of-bounds write: 16-bit truncation of outlen/inlen undersizes the chunk allocation while appends use full-size lengths
findings.anchor: outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out; / inlen = (sizeof(inreq) + stream_len) * in; and retval = sctp_make_reconf(asoc, outlen + inlen);
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow via integer truncation (size_t stored into __u16)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — sctp_make_reconf() and sctp_addto_chunk() in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c
findings.invariant: The chunk allocation size and every subsequent sctp_addto_chunk() length must be computed in an integer type wide enough to hold sizeof(param_hdr) + stream_num*sizeof(__u16) without truncation, and the request must be rejected if that total exceeds the parameter/chunk length ceiling before allocating.
findings.failure_mode: With a user-supplied stream_num near 32767 (e.g. 32767 -> stream_len 65534), sizeof(outreq)+stream_len ~ 65550 wraps to a tiny outlen (~14). sctp_make_reconf() then allocates only ~14 bytes, but sctp_addto_chunk(retval, sizeof(outreq), &outreq) writes the real ~16-byte header and sctp_addto_chunk(retval, stream_len, stream_list) writes ~65534 bytes of stream_list into it, overrunning the kernel heap allocation by tens of kilobytes — memory corruption / privilege escalation or DoS from a local SCTP_RESET_STREAMS request.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the width of stream_len/outlen/inlen (all __u16), that sctp_addto_chunk() at lines 27/30/38/41 copies the passed length without re-checking it against the chunk's remaining room, and that sctp_make_reconf()'s length argument is the sole size bound; verify whether the sctp_send_reset_streams() caller clamps stream_num before this point.

findings.title: Incorrect type conversion: user-controlled stream_num*sizeof(__u16) truncated into 16-bit stream_len
findings.bug_class: Incorrect type conversion / integer wraparound (size_t narrowed to __u16)
findings.invariant: stream_len must equal the true byte length of the stream_list (stream_num * 2) with no bit loss; the multiplication must be evaluated and stored in a type (size_t/int/u32) that cannot truncate, and stream_num must be validated against the association's stream count first.
findings.failure_mode: stream_num up to 65535 makes stream_num*2 up to 131070, which does not fit in __u16; the store discards the high bit(s) so stream_len no longer matches the real stream_list size. This corrupts every downstream length (allocation on lines 13-16, wire length on line 22/35, copy length on lines 30/41) and is the root cause of the heap overflow in finding 1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm stream_len is declared __u16 and that no earlier caller has already reduced stream_num to a safe range; check whether the copy on lines 30/41 uses this truncated stream_len while stream_list actually holds stream_num*2 bytes.

findings.title: Missing bound check on user-supplied stream_num / stream_list count
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on an attacker-influenced count
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — sctp_send_reset_streams() caller in net/sctp/stream.c
findings.invariant: stream_num must be validated against the association's number of outbound/inbound streams (and against the chunk-size ceiling) before any length arithmetic derives from it; oversized requests must be rejected with an error.
findings.failure_mode: This function performs no range check on stream_num, so if the caller path does not clamp it, an arbitrary 16-bit count flows into the truncating arithmetic and drives the overflow above. Even absent overflow, an out-of-range stream id list produces a malformed reset request.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that sctp_send_reset_streams() (or its setsockopt entry) bounds the user-supplied stream count against asoc->stream.outcnt/incnt before invoking sctp_make_strreset_req().

audit_checklist: Confirm stream_len, outlen, and inlen are all __u16 and that sizeof(outreq)+stream_len can exceed 65535 for attacker-reachable stream_num, causing truncation.
Verify sctp_make_reconf(asoc, outlen+inlen) is the only size bound and that sctp_addto_chunk() does not re-validate the copy length against the chunk's remaining capacity.
Check that the copy length on lines 30/41 (stream_len) and the header copies on lines 27/38 (real sizeof) together can exceed the allocated outlen+inlen bytes.
Confirm whether the sctp_send_reset_streams() caller clamps stream_num to the association's negotiated stream count before this builder runs.
Ensure length arithmetic is performed in size_t/int and gated by a 'reject if serialized size > SCTP chunk/param maximum' check before allocation.
Verify the wire param_hdr.length (htons(outlen)/htons(inlen)) matches the number of bytes actually appended so the emitted parameter is not malformed.
